validation.comparison_result: 4 rows expected, 4 rows actual. 2 row(s) differ with tiny floating-point rounding errors: Row 0 (sum_disc_price 537759104278.07 expected vs 537759104278.06 actual, -0.01 error), Row 2 (sum_base_price 1114302286901.88 expected vs 1114302286901.87 actual, -0.01 error). All other values match perfectly.

profiling.notes: perf tool not configured in this environment; can add with --install perf

Q1.notes: Correct row count and structure. Values accurate to 0.01 units for sums with 14-29M row contribution per group. Floating-point precision loss due to accumulation order.

Q1.bottleneck: Floating-point precision loss in accumulated sums due to order-dependent floating-point arithmetic across 14-29M rows per group
Q1.root_cause: Standard IEEE 754 double precision accumulation loses precision when summing large numbers (order of 10^11-10^12) with small increments (order of 1-100). Current implementation sums directly in aggregation loop without compensation (e.g., Kahan summation). With 59M rows passing the date filter and only 4 groups, each group accumulates 14-29M partial products, causing 0.01 ULP (unit in the last place) rounding errors.

analysis.overall: The Q1 implementation is well-optimized for iteration 1, with effective parallelism (32-48 threads), efficient columnar data access via mmap, and intelligent fixed-size hash aggregation. The primary issue is numerical precision: tiny floating-point rounding errors (±0.01) appear in 2 of 4 output rows due to accumulated error from order-dependent floating-point addition. Performance at 120ms is reasonable (2.5x faster than DuckDB min of 48ms suggests GenDB reference was unoptimized baseline). Secondary opportunity: SIMD vectorization of the filter + aggregation loop could yield 1.5-2x speedup by processing 8 rows per iteration.

critical_fixes.issue: Floating-point rounding errors (±0.01) in accumulated sums for rows 0 and 2
critical_fixes.fix: Implement Kahan summation (compensated sum) or use ordered/cascaded summation to improve precision. Alternative: use higher-precision accumulators (long double) during aggregation, convert to double in output.
critical_fixes.description: The issue manifests in sum_disc_price (row 0) and sum_base_price (row 2) where accumulated values across 14-29M rows suffer from standard floating-point rounding. Kahan summation tracks a running error term to compensate for lost precision: sum += x; error = (x - (sum - old_sum)) + error; compensated_sum = sum + error. For row 0 with 14.8M rows, each contributing 36K-40K per sum, accumulated error from standard addition causes -0.01 loss. Implementing compensated summation in lines 54-61 (AggregateState updates) will eliminate this error.

performance_optimizations.technique: SIMD Vectorization of Filter + Aggregation Loop
performance_optimizations.description: Current loop (lines 151-167) is single-row-at-a-time: for each i, load l_shipdate[i], test filter, load l_returnflag[i], l_linestatus[i], etc. This is CPU-bound with high IPC potential. Vectorize using AVX-512 (available on hardware: lscpu shows avx512f, avx512dq). Process 8 rows per iteration: load 8 l_shipdate values with _mm512_loadu_si512, compare with _mm512_cmpgt_epi32, create mask, scatter to per-group accumulators. For aggregates, use 4 double accumulators per thread per group (SIMD width = 4 for double), accumulate 4 rows at once with _mm256_add_pd. Expected speedup: 3-4x on filter + aggregation loop, 1.5-2x overall.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: Expected 1.5-2x speedup (120ms → 60-80ms). Justification: CPU bound, morsel size (32K rows) fits well in L2 (32 MiB cache per core). SIMD parallelism (AVX-512 can do 8 int32 comparisons/cycle or 4 double adds/cycle) will reduce cycles per row from ~10-15 to ~3-5.

performance_optimizations.technique: Optimized madvise Hints for HDD Access Pattern
performance_optimizations.description: Current madvise (line 82) uses MADV_SEQUENTIAL | MADV_WILLNEED for all columns uniformly. On HDD, this is suboptimal: MADV_WILLNEED triggers aggressive prefetch which can thrash if working set exceeds effective cache. For columns accessed in tight loop (l_shipdate, l_returnflag, l_linestatus, and partial l_quantity/l_extendedprice/l_discount/l_tax), use MADV_SEQUENTIAL only. For columns accessed sparsely or later (comment fields), omit madvise. Additionally, use mlock()/MADV_LOCK on hot columns to prevent OS paging. Organize mmap calls to mmap hot columns first (sequential), then large numeric columns. For 59M-row scan on HDD with 7 columns: effective bandwidth matters. Current: likely hitting disk seek limits.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: Expected 0.8-1.2x speedup (120ms → 100-120ms, marginal). Justification: HDD random seek = 5ms, sequential read = 100 MB/s. With 59M rows, 7 columns (7 * 60M * 8 bytes = 3.36 GB), theoretical time = 3.36 GB / 100 MB/s = 33.6s if fully sequential. Actual: 120ms suggests heavy caching/OS optimization. Refined madvise will reduce cache thrash, yielding small gain.

performance_optimizations.description: Current approach uses thread-local tables (line 133), then merges into global table (lines 183-203). Merge phase iterates all 6 groups across all threads sequentially. For 32+ threads, this merge can be parallelized or optimized. Alternative: Use lock-free atomic operations on global table during aggregation, avoiding merge phase. Tradeoff: atomic operations on global state can cause cache-line contention. Better approach: keep thread-local tables but use fast parallel merge with partitioned global table (each thread merges into disjoint partition to avoid contention). For 4-6 groups, contention is already low, so this optimization has diminishing returns.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: Expected 0.95-1.05x speedup (120ms → 114-126ms, negligible). Justification: Merge phase (lines 183-203) iterates 6 groups * 32 threads = 192 iterations, each doing 8 additions. Total merge cost: ~1.5K FP operations. Filter+agg loop: 59M iterations with 8 FP ops each = 472M FP ops. Merge is <0.4% of total cost.

summary: Q1 iteration 1 is a well-engineered baseline with strong parallelism (32-48 threads), efficient columnar mmap access, and tight hash aggregation for 6 groups. The critical issue is numerical precision: Kahan summation is needed to fix ±0.01 rounding errors in 2 output rows. Performance opportunity: SIMD vectorization of the main filter+aggregation loop (CPU-bound, 1.5-2x speedup expected) is the highest-ROI optimization. The code already handles the 98% selectivity filter well and should be production-ready after the precision fix. Recommendation: Implement Kahan summation immediately (low risk, fixes correctness), then add SIMD vectorization in iter_2 for performance.
